1. **Patch for the \(GL(1|1)\) specialization.** Insert the following after “Known input 1.” Let \(R_n^{(k,j)}\) denote the coinvariant ring with \(k\) bosonic and \(j\) fermionic sets. Diagonal supersymmetry gives
\[
{\rm Frob}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\lambda,\mu} c_{\lambda,\mu}\,s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)s_\mu(\mathbf z),
\]
with \(c_{\lambda,\mu}\) independent of \(k,j\). Applying the dimension map \(s_\mu(\mathbf z)\mapsto f^\mu=\dim S^\mu\) gives
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_\lambda \Big(\sum_\mu c_{\lambda,\mu}f^\mu\Big)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u).
\]
For \(j=0\), this is the original bosonic Schur expansion, so by uniqueness
\[
c_\lambda(n)=\sum_\mu c_{\lambda,\mu}f^\mu.
\]
Taking \((k,j)=(1,1)\) gives exactly
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u).
\]

2. **Patch for the superspace Hilbert product formula.** Rhoades–Wilson prove
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,z)=\sum_{k=1}^n z^{\,n-k}[k]!_q\,{\rm Stir}_q(n,k),
\]
with \({\rm Stir}_q(n,k)\) satisfying the usual Carlitz recurrence. Remmel–Wilson’s identity gives
\[
\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{{\rm maj}(w)}
\prod_{j=1}^{{\rm des}(w)}(1+z/q^j)
=
\sum_{b=0}^{n-1} z^b [n-b]!_q{\rm Stir}_q(n,n-b).
\]
Since
\[
q^{{\rm maj}(w)}\prod_{j=1}^{d}(1+z/q^j)
=
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-\binom{d+1}{2}}\prod_{j=1}^{d}(q^j+z),
\]
this is precisely the product formula used in the proof, with \(z=u\).

3. **Optional derivation of the \(1|1\) super Schur formula.** From
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u),

a one-variable Schur term \(s_\nu(q)\) forces \(\nu\) to be a row, and a one-variable skew term \(s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u)\) is nonzero only when \(\lambda'/\nu'\) is a horizontal strip. For \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), the only possible \(\nu\) are \((a)\) and \((a-1)\), giving \(q^a u^b\) and \(q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\). If \(\lambda\) is not a hook, no such surviving terms occur.

   c_\lambda(n)=d_\lambda(n)

   \sum_{w\in S_n}q^{\mathrm{maj}(w)}

   \mathrm{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)

1. **Supersymmetric universality.** Cite the diagonal-supersymmetry Hilbert-series theorem: for \(S_n\), there are coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\), independent of \((k,j)\), such that
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=\sum_{\lambda\in P(k,j,n)}c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u).
\]
When \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so these are the same coefficients as in the original bosonic Schur expansion. When \(k=j=1\), this gives the super Hilbert series used in the proof.

2. **Hook specialization.** From
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u),
\]
with one \(q\)-variable and one \(u\)-variable, only \(\nu=(a)\) and \(\nu=(a-1)\) contribute for \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\). Hence
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
The empty partition gives \(1\), and non-hooks give \(0\).

3. **Product formula from Rhoades–Wilson.** Rhoades–Wilson gives
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=\sum_{k=1}^n [k]_q!\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)u^{n-k}.
\]
To obtain the product formula used in the proof, insert the lemma
\[
\sum_{k=1}^n [k]_q!\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)u^{n-k}
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]
A standard proof is by comparing coefficients of \(u^b\). Let
\[
A_{n,d}=\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\\operatorname{des}(w)=d}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{d+1}{2}},
\qquad
P_{d,b}=q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}{d\brack b}_q .
\]
The coefficient of \(u^b\) on the product side is
\[
K_{n,b}=\sum_d A_{n,d}P_{d,b}.
\]
Carlitz’s \(q\)-Eulerian recurrence gives
\[
A_{n,d}=[d+1]_qA_{n-1,d}+[n-d]_qA_{n-1,d-1}.
\]
Using \(q\)-Pascal,
\[
P_{e+1,b}=q^{e+1-b}(P_{e,b}+P_{e,b-1})
\]
and
\[
[e+1]_qP_{e,b}=[e+1-b]_q(P_{e,b}+P_{e,b-1}).
\]
Therefore
\[
K_{n,b}=[n-b]_q(K_{n-1,b}+K_{n-1,b-1}).
\]
The coefficient of \(u^b\) in the Rhoades–Wilson \(q\)-Stirling side satisfies the same recurrence, since
\[
[k]_q!\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)
=[k]_q\bigl([k]_q!\operatorname{Stir}_q(n-1,k)
+[k-1]_q!\operatorname{Stir}_q(n-1,k-1)\bigr),
\]
with \(k=n-b\). The initial conditions agree, so the product formula follows.

1. If \(n\) is inserted after a descent \(i_j\), then the descent at \(i_j\) is replaced by one at \(i_j+1\), and descents to its right shift by \(1\). The number of descents remains \(e\), and the major-index increase is \(e-j+1\). Insertion at the final gap has increase \(0\). Thus descent-preserving insertions contribute \(1+q+\cdots+q^e=[e+1]_q\).

2. If \(n\) is inserted before the first entry or after an ascent, the number of descents increases to \(e+1\). Before the first entry, the major-index increase is \(e+1\), exactly canceled by the normalization change
\[
\binom{e+2}{2}-\binom{e+1}{2}=e+1.
\]
After an ascent at position \(i\), if \(r=\#\{h\in\mathrm{Des}(v):h\le i\}\), then the major-index increase is \(i+1+(e-r)\), so the normalized exponent increase is
\[
i+1+e-r-(e+1)=i-r,
\]
the number of ascents among positions \(\le i\). As the ascents are read left to right, these shifts are \(1,\ldots,n-e-2\), together with \(0\) from the initial gap. Hence descent-increasing insertions contribute \([n-e-1]_q\).

Let \({\rm Stir}_q(n,k)\) be defined by
\[
{\rm Stir}_q(n,k)={\rm Stir}_q(n-1,k-1)+[k]_q{\rm Stir}_q(n-1,k),
\qquad {\rm Stir}_q(0,k)=\delta_{0,k}.
\]
For \(0\le b\le n\), set
\[
O_{n,b}=[n-b]_q!\,{\rm Stir}_q(n,n-b)
\]
and
\[
E_{n,b}=\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(w)\brack b}_q .
\]
Then \(O_{n,b}=E_{n,b}\).

Proof: From the \(q\)-Stirling recurrence,
\[
O_{n,b}=[n-b]_q\bigl(O_{n-1,b}+O_{n-1,b-1}\bigr),
\]
with the usual convention that out-of-range terms are zero.

Now prove the same recurrence for \(E_{n,b}\) by inserting \(n\) into a permutation \(v\in S_{n-1}\). Write \(e={\rm des}(v)\) and \(M={\rm maj}(v)\). The \(e+1\) slots after a descent of \(v\), together with the terminal slot, keep the descent number \(e\); their major-index increments are \(0,1,\dots,e\). Their total contribution is
\[
q^{M-be+\binom b2}{{e}\brack b}_q [e+1]_q.
\]
The remaining \(n-1-e\) slots increase the descent number to \(e+1\); their major-index increments are \(e+1,e+2,\dots,n-1\). Their total contribution is
\[
q^{M-be+\binom b2}\,
q^{e+1-b}[n-e-1]_q{{e+1}\brack b}_q.
\]
Thus the total contribution of \(v\) to \(E_{n,b}\) is
\[
q^{M-be+\binom b2}
\left(
[e+1]_q{{e}\brack b}_q
+
q^{e+1-b}[n-e-1]_q{{e+1}\brack b}_q
\right).
\]
Using
\[
[e+1]_q{{e}\brack b}_q=[e+1-b]_q{{e+1}\brack b}_q
\]
and \([r]_q+q^r[s]_q=[r+s]_q\), the bracket equals
\[
[n-b]_q{{e+1}\brack b}_q.
\]
Then \(q\)-Pascal gives
\[
{{e+1}\brack b}_q={{e}\brack b}_q+q^{e+1-b}{{e}\brack b-1}_q,
\]
so the contribution equals \([n-b]_q\) times the contribution of \(v\) to \(E_{n-1,b}+E_{n-1,b-1}\). Hence
\[
E_{n,b}=[n-b]_q(E_{n-1,b}+E_{n-1,b-1}).
\]
The initial conditions agree, so \(E_{n,b}=O_{n,b}\).

Therefore the Rhoades–Wilson formula
\[
\sum_{k=0}^n u^{n-k}[k]_q!\,{\rm Stir}_q(n,k)
\]
has \(u^b\)-coefficient \(E_{n,b}\), which is exactly the \(u^b\)-coefficient of
\[

q^{{\rm maj}(w)-\binom{{\rm des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{{\rm des}(w)}(q^i+u).

Let \(v\in S_{N-1}\) have descent set \(D=\{i_1<\cdots<i_e\}\) and major index \(M\). Insert \(N\) in the slot after position \(r\), with \(r=0\) meaning before the first letter and \(r=N-1\) meaning the terminal slot.

- If \(r=N-1\), the descent set is unchanged and the major-index increment is \(0\).
- If \(r\in D\), the descent at \(r\) is replaced by a descent at \(r+1\), and descents after \(r\) shift right by one. Thus the descent number stays \(e\), and the major-index increment is
\[
1+\#\{i\in D:i>r\}.
\]
As \(r\) runs over descents from right to left, these increments are \(1,\ldots,e\), together with terminal increment \(0\).
- If \(r\notin D\) and \(0\le r\le N-2\), insertion creates a new descent at \(r+1\), and descents after \(r\) shift right by one. Thus the descent number becomes \(e+1\), and the increment is
\[
r+1+\#\{i\in D:i>r\}
= e+1+\bigl(r-\#\{i\in D:i\le r\}\bigr).
\]
As \(r\) runs over the \(N-1-e\) non-descent slots before the terminal slot, the last parenthesis runs through \(0,\ldots,N-2-e\), so the increments are \(e+1,\ldots,N-1\).

Using
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u),

Since \(\nu=(r)\) removes \(r\) boxes from the first row of \(\lambda\), \(\lambda/\nu\) can be a vertical strip only if every lower row of \(\lambda\) has length at most \(1\). Hence \(\lambda\) is a hook. For \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), the only possible \(r\) are \(r=a\) and \(r=a-1\), giving respectively
\[
q^a u^b,\qquad q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]

s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1},

and non-hooks contribute \(0\).

For \(b,t\ge0\),
\[
{b+t\brack b}_q=\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times t}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
This is the standard Ferrers-diagram/lattice-path interpretation of Gaussian binomial coefficients: partitions in a \(b\times t\) rectangle correspond to binary words with \(b\) north steps and \(t\) east steps, with area/inversion statistic equal to \(|\rho|\). Taking \(t=d-1-b\) gives
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q=\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.

\[

\[
\frac1{n!}\sum_{\sigma\in\mathfrak S_n}\sigma

\[

=
(A_L^{\mathfrak S_n})_+A_L.
